concepts.body: A distributed system can only guarantee two of three: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance. Most systems choose AP (eventually consistent) or CP (consistent but may be unavailable).

concepts.title: Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling
concepts.body: Vertical: bigger machine. Horizontal: more machines. Horizontal is preferred for availability and cost at scale — but requires stateless services and distributed data.

concepts.title: Latency vs Throughput
concepts.body: Latency is time for one request. Throughput is requests per second. Optimising one often harms the other — batch processing improves throughput but increases individual latency.

concepts.title: Back-of-Envelope Estimation
concepts.body: Estimate QPS, storage, bandwidth before designing. 1M DAU × 10 req/day ÷ 86400 ≈ 115 QPS. Numbers ground decisions about caching, sharding, and server count.

resources.label: System Design Primer
resources.desc: The most-starred system design resource on GitHub — scalability, caching, and more.

resources.label: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
resources.desc: The bible of distributed systems — replication, sharding, consistency, consensus.

resources.label: ByteByteGo
resources.desc: Visual system design explanations loved by engineers worldwide.

resources.label: High Scalability Blog
resources.desc: Real-world architecture teardowns of Twitter, YouTube, Amazon, and more.

resources.label: AWS Architecture Center
resources.desc: Reference architectures and best practices for scalable cloud systems.

checklist.label: Functional and non-functional requirements clarified before designing
Back-of-envelope estimates done (QPS, storage, bandwidth)
Single points of failure identified and eliminated
Caching strategy defined (what to cache, TTL, invalidation)
Database choice justified — SQL vs NoSQL vs hybrid
Async processing used for long-running tasks (queues, workers)
Monitoring, alerting, and runbooks defined
Failover and disaster recovery strategy documented

concepts.title: Load Balancers
concepts.body: Distribute traffic across multiple servers. Layer 4 (TCP) for raw throughput; Layer 7 (HTTP) for content-aware routing. Use health checks to remove unhealthy instances automatically.

concepts.title: Stateless Services
concepts.body: Services that store no session data locally can be scaled horizontally with zero configuration. Move all session state to Redis or a database — any instance can then handle any request.

concepts.title: Auto-Scaling
concepts.body: Automatically add or remove instances based on load. Use CPU, memory, or custom metrics (queue depth, request latency) as triggers. Design for both scale-out and scale-in.

concepts.title: CDN
concepts.body: Serve static assets and cached responses from edge nodes close to users. Offloads origin servers dramatically. Cache-Control headers determine what CDNs store and for how long.

resources.label: The System Design Primer — Scalability
resources.desc: Scalability chapter from the most-starred system design repo.

concepts.title: Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading)
concepts.body: Application checks cache first. On miss, reads from DB and populates cache. Simple and resilient to cache failures. Risk: cache misses on new data cause DB spikes.

concepts.title: Write-Through
concepts.body: Write to cache and DB simultaneously on every update. Data is always fresh, but adds write latency. Good for read-heavy workloads with low write volume.

concepts.title: Cache Eviction Policies
concepts.body: LRU (Least Recently Used) is the default. LFU (Least Frequently Used) better for skewed access patterns. TTL-based expiry is mandatory to prevent stale data accumulation.

concepts.title: Cache Stampede
concepts.body: Many requests hit the DB simultaneously on cache expiry. Mitigate with mutex locks, staggered TTLs, or probabilistic early expiration (re-compute before TTL expires).

resources.label: Redis Documentation
resources.desc: Full Redis reference — data types, persistence, clustering, pub/sub.

concepts.title: Message Queues
concepts.body: Decouple producers from consumers. Producers write to the queue; consumers process at their own pace. Buffers load spikes and allows independent scaling of each side.

concepts.title: Pub/Sub
concepts.body: Publishers broadcast messages to a topic. Multiple subscribers consume independently. Unlike queues, each subscriber gets a copy — used for fan-out, event broadcasting, and notifications.

concepts.title: At-Least-Once Delivery
concepts.body: Messages are guaranteed to be delivered but may be delivered more than once. Consumers must be idempotent — processing the same message twice must produce the same result.

concepts.title: Dead Letter Queues
concepts.body: Failed messages land in a DLQ after max retries. Prevents poison messages from blocking the queue. Inspect and replay from DLQ after fixing the underlying issue.

resources.label: Apache Kafka Docs
resources.desc: The de-facto standard for distributed event streaming.

resources.label: RabbitMQ Tutorials
resources.desc: Hands-on queue patterns — work queues, pub/sub, routing, and RPC.

concepts.title: Database Sharding
concepts.body: Horizontally partition data across multiple DB nodes. Each shard holds a subset of rows. Choose a shard key that distributes load evenly and keeps related data on the same shard.

concepts.title: Replication
concepts.body: Leader-follower: writes go to leader, reads from followers. Improves read throughput and availability. Trade-off: replication lag means followers may serve stale data.

concepts.title: SQL vs NoSQL
concepts.body: SQL: strong consistency, ACID, joins, well-understood. NoSQL: flexible schema, horizontal scale, eventual consistency. Choose based on access patterns, not hype.

concepts.title: Object Storage
concepts.body: Store blobs (images, videos, backups) in S3-compatible stores. Infinitely scalable, cheap, and globally available. Serve directly via CDN — never proxy through your app servers.

resources.label: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
resources.desc: Replication, partitioning, transactions, and distributed consensus.

concepts.title: API Gateway
concepts.body: Single entry point that routes, authenticates, rate-limits, and transforms requests. Hides internal service topology from clients and enables cross-cutting concerns centrally.

concepts.title: REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC
concepts.body: REST: simple, widely understood, HTTP caching. GraphQL: flexible queries, single endpoint, great for complex UIs. gRPC: binary, typed, high-performance — ideal for internal service calls.

concepts.title: Idempotency
concepts.body: Ensure repeated identical requests have the same effect as a single request. Use idempotency keys for payment and order APIs. GET/PUT/DELETE are idempotent; POST is not by default.

concepts.title: Versioning
concepts.body: Version your APIs from day one — /v1/, /v2/ in the URL or via headers. Never make breaking changes to an existing version. Deprecate with a sunset date and migration guide.

resources.label: REST API Design Rulebook
resources.desc: Principles for designing consistent, intuitive REST APIs at scale.

microservices.label: Microservices
microservices.description: Design, build, and operate distributed microservice architectures — decomposition, communication, resilience, data management, and observability.

concepts.title: Single Responsibility at Service Level
concepts.body: Each service owns one business capability end-to-end. If two services always change together, they should be one service. If one service needs another's data for every request, reconsider the split.

concepts.title: Decentralised Data Management
concepts.body: Each service owns its own database. No shared databases between services — they create hidden coupling that defeats the purpose of microservices.

concepts.title: Design for Failure
concepts.body: Network calls fail. Services go down. Every inter-service call must have a timeout, retry policy, and a fallback. The system must degrade gracefully, not cascade.

concepts.title: Operational Overhead
concepts.body: Microservices add distributed systems complexity. You need service discovery, distributed tracing, contract testing, and deployment pipelines per service. Start with a modular monolith.

resources.label: Building Microservices
resources.desc: Sam Newman — the definitive guide to decomposition, communication, and deployment.

resources.label: Microservices Patterns
resources.desc: Chris Richardson — Saga, CQRS, API Gateway, Service Mesh patterns with trade-off analysis.

resources.label: microservices.io
resources.desc: The canonical pattern catalog for microservices.

resources.label: Monolith to Microservices
resources.desc: Strangler fig, branch by abstraction, and parallel change strategies.

checklist.label: Service boundaries aligned with business capabilities
Each service has its own isolated database
All inter-service calls have timeouts and retry policies
Circuit breakers implemented for critical dependencies
Distributed tracing with correlation IDs across all services
Consumer-driven contract tests between services
Health checks and readiness probes on every service
Each service has an independent CI/CD pipeline

concepts.title: Decompose by Business Capability
concepts.body: Identify the business capabilities your organisation performs. Each capability (Order Management, Payment, Notification) maps to a service. Aligns services with organisational structure.

concepts.title: Decompose by Subdomain (DDD)
concepts.body: Use Domain-Driven Design to identify core, supporting, and generic subdomains. Core subdomains get the most attention; generic ones use off-the-shelf solutions.

concepts.title: Strangler Fig Pattern
concepts.body: Gradually replace a monolith by routing specific requests to new microservices. The monolith shrinks over time. Never do a big-bang rewrite — it almost always fails.

concepts.title: Avoiding Distributed Monolith
concepts.body: Services that must all be deployed together or that synchronously call each other in a chain are a distributed monolith — the worst of both worlds. Design for independence.

resources.label: Monolith to Microservices — Sam Newman
resources.desc: Step-by-step decomposition strategies with real patterns.

concepts.title: Synchronous vs Asynchronous
concepts.body: Synchronous (HTTP, gRPC) is simpler but creates temporal coupling — if the downstream service is slow or down, the caller is too. Async (events, queues) decouples availability but adds complexity.

concepts.title: gRPC for Internal Communication
concepts.body: gRPC is binary, typed, and fast — ideal for high-throughput internal service calls. Auto-generates client SDKs from protobuf definitions. Built-in bidirectional streaming.

concepts.title: Service Mesh
concepts.body: Offload cross-cutting concerns (mTLS, retries, tracing, load balancing) to a sidecar proxy (Envoy/Istio). Services communicate as if on a reliable network — the mesh handles the rest.

concepts.title: API Contracts
concepts.body: Define strict API contracts using OpenAPI or protobuf. Use consumer-driven contract testing (Pact) to ensure providers never break their consumers unintentionally.

resources.label: gRPC Documentation
resources.desc: High-performance RPC framework for inter-service communication.

resources.label: Istio Service Mesh Docs
resources.desc: Traffic management, mTLS, observability for microservice fleets.

concepts.title: Circuit Breaker
concepts.body: When failure rate exceeds a threshold, open the circuit — fail fast instead of waiting for timeouts. After a cooldown period, allow a test request through. Prevents cascading failures.

concepts.title: Retry with Exponential Backoff
concepts.body: Retry transient failures with increasing delay between attempts (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s) plus jitter. Without jitter, all retries hit the downstream service simultaneously — a thundering herd.

concepts.title: Bulkhead
concepts.body: Isolate resources (thread pools, connection pools) per downstream dependency. A slow database shouldn't exhaust all threads and starve other operations. Fail one compartment, not the ship.

concepts.title: Graceful Degradation
concepts.body: When a dependency fails, serve a reduced but functional experience. Return cached data, a default response, or a partial result — never a blank page or unhandled exception.

resources.label: Resilience4j Docs
resources.desc: Lightweight fault tolerance library — circuit breaker, retry, bulkhead.

concepts.title: Saga Pattern
concepts.body: Manage distributed transactions as a sequence of local transactions. Choreography: services react to events. Orchestration: a saga orchestrator tells services what to do and handles failures.

concepts.title: Eventual Consistency
concepts.body: Distributed systems cannot have strong consistency without sacrificing availability. Accept that replicas may diverge temporarily — design UX to handle stale reads gracefully.

concepts.title: Database per Service
concepts.body: Each service owns its schema and data. Other services query via API, never direct DB access. Enables independent schema evolution, different DB engines, and clear ownership.

concepts.title: API Composition
concepts.body: For queries that join data across services, have a dedicated aggregator service or gateway call each service and compose the result. Avoid cross-service joins at the database level.

resources.label: Microservices Patterns — Chris Richardson
resources.desc: Saga, CQRS, and data management patterns with trade-off analysis.

concepts.title: Three Pillars
concepts.body: Metrics (aggregated numbers — latency p99, error rate), Logs (timestamped events), Traces (request paths across services). You need all three — each answers different questions.

concepts.title: Distributed Tracing
concepts.body: Propagate a correlation ID through every service call. Visualise the full request path, identify latency contributors, and debug errors across service boundaries without grep.

concepts.title: OpenTelemetry
concepts.body: Vendor-neutral SDK for capturing traces, metrics, and logs. Instrument once, export to any backend (Jaeger, Grafana Tempo, Datadog). Becoming the industry standard.

concepts.title: SLIs, SLOs, and SLAs
concepts.body: SLI: a metric you measure (request success rate). SLO: your internal target (99.9% success). SLA: the contract with customers. Design monitoring around SLO burn rate, not raw thresholds.

resources.label: OpenTelemetry Documentation
resources.desc: Vendor-neutral observability framework for traces, metrics, and logs.

resources.label: Distributed Systems Observability
resources.desc: Free O'Reilly book — metrics, logging, and tracing in distributed systems.

security.label: Security
security.description: Protect applications from vulnerabilities — from OWASP Top 10 to authentication, cryptography, API security, and infrastructure hardening.

concepts.title: Defence in Depth
concepts.body: Layer multiple security controls so that when one fails, another catches it. No single mechanism is sufficient — validate input at every layer, not just the edge.

concepts.title: Principle of Least Privilege
concepts.body: Grant only the permissions required to perform a task and no more. A database user for the API should not have DROP TABLE rights. A Lambda function should not have full S3 access.

concepts.title: Assume Breach
concepts.body: Design as if attackers will eventually get in. Segment networks, encrypt data at rest and in transit, log everything, and implement detection — not just prevention.

concepts.title: Shift Left on Security
concepts.body: Find vulnerabilities in development, not production. Run SAST/DAST in CI, review dependencies for CVEs on every PR, and threat-model new features before writing a line of code.

resources.label: OWASP Top 10
resources.desc: The essential checklist of the 10 most critical web application security risks.

resources.label: PortSwigger Web Security Academy
resources.desc: Free, hands-on labs for every major web vulnerability — the best practical security training.

resources.label: OWASP Cheat Sheet Series
resources.desc: Quick-reference guides for securing authentication, sessions, APIs, and cryptography.

resources.label: Security Headers
resources.desc: Scan any URL and grade its HTTP security headers — CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options.

checklist.label: OWASP Top 10 reviewed and mitigations applied
All user input validated and sanitized server-side
Parameterized queries used everywhere — no SQL string concatenation
Security headers set: HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy
Passwords hashed with bcrypt/argon2 — never MD5 or SHA-1
Secrets in environment variables / vault — never in source code
Dependencies audited for CVEs on every build (npm audit / snyk)
HTTPS enforced everywhere; HTTP redirects to HTTPS
Rate limiting on all auth and sensitive endpoints
Security events logged and monitored with alerts

concepts.title: SQL Injection
concepts.body: Attacker injects SQL via input fields to read, modify, or delete data. Prevention: parameterized queries / prepared statements everywhere. ORM does not automatically protect you.

concepts.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
concepts.body: Attacker injects malicious scripts into pages viewed by other users. Prevention: escape output, use Content Security Policy, avoid innerHTML with user data, validate on server side.

concepts.title: CSRF
concepts.body: Cross-Site Request Forgery — tricks a logged-in user's browser into making an unwanted request. Prevention: CSRF tokens, SameSite cookie attribute, verifying Origin/Referer headers.

concepts.title: Broken Access Control
concepts.body: OWASP #1 — users accessing data they shouldn't. Enforce authorization checks on every endpoint, not just the UI. Test by changing IDs in requests (IDOR — Insecure Direct Object Reference).

concepts.title: Security Misconfiguration
concepts.body: Default credentials, debug modes in production, overly permissive CORS, directory listing enabled. Automate configuration checks with security scanners in your CI pipeline.

resources.label: OWASP Top 10
resources.desc: The 10 most critical web application security risks.

resources.label: PortSwigger Web Security Academy
resources.desc: Free hands-on labs for every major web vulnerability.

concepts.title: Password Hashing
concepts.body: Use bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2. Never MD5 or SHA-1 — they're not designed for passwords. Add a salt (built into bcrypt). Use a cost factor that makes cracking impractical.

concepts.title: JWT Best Practices
concepts.body: Sign with RS256 (asymmetric) in production. Validate issuer, audience, and expiry on every request. Store in httpOnly cookies, not localStorage (XSS resistant). Keep payloads small.

concepts.title: OAuth 2.0 & OIDC
concepts.body: OAuth 2.0 is for authorization (access to resources). OIDC adds authentication (identity). Use the Authorization Code flow with PKCE for web and mobile — never Implicit flow.

concepts.title: RBAC / ABAC
concepts.body: Role-Based Access Control assigns permissions to roles, not users. ABAC evaluates attributes (user, resource, environment) for fine-grained decisions. Enforce at the service layer, not just UI.

concepts.title: Multi-Factor Authentication
concepts.body: Something you know (password) + something you have (TOTP app) + something you are (biometrics). Even weak passwords become safe with MFA — enforce it for all admin accounts.

resources.label: OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet
resources.desc: Complete guide to secure authentication implementation.

resources.label: JWT.io
resources.desc: Decode, verify, and generate JWT tokens — with library references.

concepts.title: Symmetric vs Asymmetric Encryption
concepts.body: Symmetric (AES): same key encrypts and decrypts — fast, used for bulk data. Asymmetric (RSA/ECC): public key encrypts, private key decrypts — used for key exchange and signatures.

concepts.title: TLS / HTTPS
concepts.body: TLS authenticates the server, negotiates a symmetric key, and encrypts all traffic. Always use TLS 1.2+. Disable weak ciphers. Use HSTS to prevent downgrade attacks.

concepts.title: Hashing vs Encryption
concepts.body: Hashing is one-way (SHA-256, bcrypt). Encryption is reversible. Use hashing for passwords and integrity checks. Use encryption for data you need to retrieve (tokens, PII, secrets).

concepts.title: Key Management
concepts.body: Never hardcode keys. Rotate them regularly. Use a dedicated secret store (AWS KMS, HashiCorp Vault). Separate keys per environment. Audit every key access.

resources.label: Crypto 101 (free book)
resources.desc: Free cryptography primer — block ciphers, hashing, TLS, and public-key infrastructure.

resources.label: SSL Labs Server Test
resources.desc: Grade your TLS configuration and find weak cipher suites.

concepts.title: OWASP API Security Top 10
concepts.body: API-specific risks: Broken Object Level Authorization, Excessive Data Exposure, Lack of Resources & Rate Limiting, Broken Function Level Authorization. Different from the classic OWASP Top 10.

concepts.title: Rate Limiting & Throttling
concepts.body: Limit requests per IP and per API key. Use token bucket or sliding window algorithms. Return 429 with Retry-After header. Protect auth endpoints most aggressively.

concepts.title: Input Validation on Every Endpoint
concepts.body: Validate all inputs — type, length, format, range — at the server, not just the frontend. Use schema validation libraries (Zod, Joi). Reject malformed requests before business logic runs.

concepts.title: CORS Configuration
concepts.body: Don't use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on authenticated endpoints. Whitelist specific origins. CORS is a browser control — it doesn't protect server-to-server API calls.

resources.label: OWASP API Security Top 10
resources.desc: The API-specific version of the OWASP Top 10.

resources.label: OWASP API Security Cheat Sheet
resources.desc: REST API security best practices from OWASP.

concepts.title: Least Privilege IAM
concepts.body: Cloud IAM roles should grant only the permissions needed. Avoid wildcard permissions (*). Use service accounts per workload. Audit permission usage with Cloud IAM analyser.

concepts.title: Network Segmentation
concepts.body: Put databases and internal services in private subnets — not publicly accessible. Use security groups or firewall rules to allow only required traffic between services.

concepts.title: Secrets Management
concepts.body: Never commit secrets to version control. Use a dedicated vault (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Rotate secrets regularly. Audit every secret access. Scan repos for leaked secrets.

concepts.title: Patch Management
concepts.body: Run automated vulnerability scans on container images and dependencies. Use minimal base images (Alpine, Distroless). Rebuild and redeploy images regularly — don't SSH into running containers.

resources.label: HackTheBox
resources.desc: Hands-on security labs — CTF challenges and career paths for developers.

resources.label: AWS Security Best Practices
resources.desc: AWS security documentation — IAM, VPC, encryption, compliance.

performance.label: Performance
performance.description: Measure and improve application speed at every layer — frontend rendering, backend throughput, database queries, and network delivery.

concepts.title: Measure Before Optimising
concepts.body: Premature optimisation is the root of all evil. Profile first — find the actual bottleneck. Gut feelings are wrong more often than not. A 5% improvement on a non-bottleneck changes nothing.

concepts.title: Amdahl's Law
concepts.body: Speedup from parallelising is limited by the sequential fraction. If 20% of work is sequential, max speedup is 5× regardless of how many cores you add. Optimise the bottleneck.

concepts.title: Latency Numbers Every Dev Should Know
concepts.body: L1 cache: 1ns. RAM: 100ns. SSD: 100µs. Network round-trip: 1–100ms. These orders-of-magnitude guide architecture — avoid a DB call where a cache lookup will do.

concepts.title: The 80/20 Rule
concepts.body: 80% of performance gain comes from 20% of optimisations. Find the slow path (using profilers and tracing) and fix that — don't micro-optimise code that runs 0.1% of the time.

resources.label: High Performance Browser Networking
resources.desc: TCP, TLS, HTTP/2, WebSockets, WebRTC — free book by Ilya Grigorik.

resources.label: web.dev — Performance
resources.desc: Google's authoritative guide to Core Web Vitals, lazy loading, and caching.

resources.label: The USE Method
resources.desc: Utilization, Saturation, Errors — a systematic framework for diagnosing bottlenecks.

resources.label: WebPageTest
resources.desc: Deep performance testing from real browsers in real locations — waterfall, filmstrip.

checklist.label: Production profiling done before any optimisation
Core Web Vitals pass: LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, INP < 200ms
Static assets served with long Cache-Control max-age via CDN
Slow queries identified with EXPLAIN and indexes added
N+1 query problem eliminated — use joins or DataLoader batching
JS bundle analysed — code split, tree-shaken, no duplicate deps
Images served as WebP/AVIF with correct sizes and lazy loading
Gzip/Brotli compression enabled on server responses

concepts.title: Core Web Vitals
concepts.body: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint < 2.5s): page load speed. CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift < 0.1): visual stability. INP (Interaction to Next Paint < 200ms): responsiveness. Google uses these for ranking.

concepts.title: Critical Rendering Path
concepts.body: HTML → DOM → CSSOM → Render Tree → Layout → Paint → Composite. Blocking resources (render-blocking scripts, large CSS) delay this. Preload critical resources, defer non-critical scripts.

concepts.title: Code Splitting & Lazy Loading
concepts.body: Split your JS bundle by route. Lazy-load components not needed on initial render. React.lazy + Suspense, dynamic import(). Don't ship code users may never run.

concepts.title: Image Optimisation
concepts.body: Use WebP/AVIF (40–60% smaller than JPEG). Set explicit width/height to prevent CLS. Use srcset for responsive images. Lazy-load below-the-fold images with loading='lazy'.

concepts.title: Perceived Performance
concepts.body: Skeleton screens, optimistic UI updates, and instant feedback make apps feel faster than they are. Show something immediately — even a spinner is better than a blank screen.

resources.label: web.dev — Performance
resources.desc: Core Web Vitals, rendering, and browser performance from Google.

resources.label: Chrome DevTools Performance
resources.desc: How to profile, flame charts, long tasks, and memory leaks.

resources.label: Lighthouse Docs
resources.desc: Automated auditing for performance, accessibility, SEO, and best practices.

concepts.title: Profiling First
concepts.body: Use a profiler (py-spy, clinic.js, pprof) to find where CPU time is spent. Flame graphs show the call stack. Fix the widest bars — small optimisations elsewhere are noise.

concepts.title: Concurrency Models
concepts.body: Thread-per-request (traditional): simple but memory-intensive at scale. Async/event-loop (Node, Go): high concurrency with low overhead. CPU-bound work belongs in worker threads or processes.

concepts.title: Caching
concepts.body: Cache expensive computations and frequent DB queries in Redis with appropriate TTLs. Even a 1-second cache on a hot endpoint can eliminate thousands of DB calls per minute.

concepts.title: Connection Pooling
concepts.body: Opening a DB connection is expensive. Pool connections and reuse them. Set pool size based on DB thread limit and server concurrency. Too large a pool is as bad as too small.

resources.label: Systems Performance — Brendan Gregg
resources.desc: CPU, memory, storage, and network performance analysis using flame graphs.

concepts.title: EXPLAIN ANALYZE
concepts.body: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on slow queries. Look for Seq Scan on large tables (needs an index), high row estimates, nested loops on large datasets. This is the most valuable performance tool you have.

concepts.title: Indexing Strategy
concepts.body: Index columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY. Composite indexes: put the most selective column first. Partial indexes for frequently queried subsets. Too many indexes slow writes.

concepts.title: N+1 Query Problem
concepts.body: Fetching a list of N records and then querying for related data N times. Fix with JOIN, eager loading, or DataLoader batching. EXPLAIN reveals it; query logging confirms it.

concepts.title: Query Optimisation Patterns
concepts.body: Select only needed columns — never SELECT *. Use pagination (LIMIT/OFFSET or cursor-based). Avoid functions on indexed columns in WHERE clauses — they prevent index use.

resources.label: Use The Index, Luke
resources.desc: SQL performance explained — how indexes really work, query plans, and tuning.

resources.label: PostgreSQL EXPLAIN documentation
resources.desc: Official guide to reading query plans in Postgres.

concepts.title: HTTP/2 & HTTP/3
concepts.body: HTTP/2 multiplexes requests over a single connection — eliminates head-of-line blocking and makes domain sharding counterproductive. HTTP/3 (QUIC) improves performance on lossy networks.

concepts.title: Compression
concepts.body: Gzip/Brotli compress text responses (HTML, CSS, JS, JSON) by 70–90%. Brotli compresses ~20% better than Gzip. Never compress already-compressed formats (JPEG, PNG, MP4).

concepts.title: CDN Strategy
concepts.body: Serve static assets with far-future Cache-Control max-age via CDN. Cache API responses at the edge for public data. Use cache invalidation rather than short TTLs.

concepts.title: DNS & TLS Optimisation
concepts.body: Preconnect to third-party origins (<link rel='preconnect'>). Use TLS 1.3 for 1-RTT handshakes. Enable OCSP stapling to eliminate certificate revocation round-trips.

resources.label: High Performance Browser Networking
resources.desc: TCP, TLS, HTTP/2, WebSockets — how the network works and how to optimize it.

resources.label: WebPageTest
resources.desc: Deep performance testing from real browsers and locations.

soft.label: Soft Skills
soft.description: The human side of engineering — communication, leadership, and emotional intelligence.

checklist.label: Meeting has clear agenda & desired outcome
Feedback is specific, behavioral, and timely
Deep work blocks scheduled in calendar
Repeat back understanding before disagreeing
Key decisions documented in writing
Delegate the 'what', not the 'how'

concepts.title: Active Listening
concepts.body: Repeat back what you heard before responding. Ask clarifying questions. In code review, comment on intent before style.

concepts.title: Async Writing
concepts.body: Lead with the conclusion (BLUF). Use bullet points for scanability. Record decisions and 'why' — future you will thank you.

concepts.title: Technical Storytelling
concepts.body: Frame trade-offs as 'option A buys X but costs Y'. Use diagrams for systems. Anchor numbers (latency, cost) before opinions.

concepts.title: Stakeholder Translation
concepts.body: Translate engineering jargon into business outcomes. 'Refactor' → 'reduces incident frequency'. 'Tech debt' → 'slowing feature velocity 30%'.

concepts.title: 1:1 Conversations
concepts.body: Show up with one topic, not a status report. Ask open questions ('what's draining you?'). Silence is a tool — let people finish their thought.

concepts.title: Disagree & Commit
concepts.body: State your objection once, clearly, with evidence. If the team chooses otherwise, commit fully — no sabotage, no 'I told you so'.

concepts.title: Written Standups
concepts.body: Yesterday / Today / Blockers — kept under 5 lines. Tag people you need from. Async beats a 30-minute meeting for 6 people.

resources.label: Nonviolent Communication
resources.desc: The foundational framework for empathy.

resources.label: Crucial Conversations
resources.desc: Tools for talking when stakes are high.

resources.label: Never Split the Difference
resources.desc: Tactical empathy and negotiation by an ex-FBI hostage negotiator.

concepts.title: Open With a Hook
concepts.body: First 30 seconds decide whether the room listens. Start with a surprising number, a sharp question, or a 1-sentence story — never with 'My name is...'.

concepts.title: Rule of Three
concepts.body: Group ideas in threes: 3 problems, 3 reasons, 3 takeaways. The brain remembers triplets and forgets lists of seven.

concepts.title: Slow Down, Then Slow Down Again
concepts.body: Nerves push you to 1.5x speed. Mark deliberate pauses on your slides. A 2-second silence after a key point lands harder than any animation.

concepts.title: Demo Discipline
concepts.body: Record a backup video. Pre-stage data. Zoom your terminal to 18pt+. Never type a password on stage — paste from a notes file.

concepts.title: Q&A Without Panic
concepts.body: Repeat the question (buys time, helps the room). 'I don't know — I'll follow up' is a strong answer. Park hostile threads: 'great topic, let's chat after'.

resources.label: Talk Like TED
resources.desc: Watch 3 talks in your domain, copy what works, drop what doesn't.

resources.label: Presentation Zen
resources.desc: Slide design philosophy that kills bullet-heavy decks.

concepts.title: Anchor First
concepts.body: Whoever names the first number shapes the range. Research market data, then open at the high end of reasonable.

concepts.title: Trade, Don't Concede
concepts.body: Never give without getting. 'I can do that deadline if scope drops feature X' beats 'sure, no problem'.

concepts.title: BATNA
concepts.body: Know your Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement. If you have no walk-away option, you're not negotiating — you're begging.

concepts.title: Lead Without Authority
concepts.body: Influence by demonstrating, not directing. Write the first RFC. Build the smallest reference implementation. Volunteer for the boring glue.

concepts.title: Delegation
concepts.body: Delegate outcomes, not steps. Give context, constraints, and a deadline. Trust the person to choose the path.

concepts.title: Run Effective 1:1s
concepts.body: Their agenda first, yours second. Weekly cadence, 30 minutes. Notes in a shared doc you both edit — career, blockers, feedback.

concepts.title: Radical Candor
concepts.body: Care personally + challenge directly. Praise in public, critique in private, always specific and timely.

resources.label: Extreme Ownership
resources.desc: Leadership principles from Navy SEALs.

resources.label: The Manager's Path
resources.desc: Guide for tech leaders.

resources.label: Radical Candor
resources.desc: Kim Scott's framework for honest, caring feedback.

concepts.title: Time-Blocking
concepts.body: Assign every hour a job before the day starts. Deep work goes in your peak energy window (morning for most). Shallow tasks — email, Slack, meetings — fill the gaps. A blank calendar is not free time, it's stolen time.

concepts.title: Eisenhower Matrix
concepts.body: Split tasks into 4 quadrants: Urgent+Important (do now), Important+Not Urgent (schedule), Urgent+Not Important (delegate), Neither (eliminate). Most engineers live in quadrant 1 — the goal is to spend more time in quadrant 2.

concepts.title: Eat the Frog
concepts.body: Do your hardest, most important task first — before email, before Slack. Once the frog is eaten, every other task feels easy. Procrastination feeds on easy wins.

concepts.title: Pomodoro Technique
concepts.body: 25 minutes of focused work, 5-minute break, repeat 4×, then take a longer 20-minute break. Forces single-tasking. The break is not optional — it's when your brain consolidates.

concepts.title: Single-Tasking
concepts.body: Multitasking is a myth. Context-switching costs ~23 minutes of recovery per interruption. Close unrelated tabs. Put your phone face down. Batch your communication windows.

concepts.title: Weekly Review
concepts.body: Every Friday: review what shipped, what didn't, and why. Reschedule incomplete high-priority tasks before Monday. 30 minutes of weekly planning saves 5 hours of reactive scrambling.

checklist.label: Next week's deep work blocks scheduled
Most important task identified for tomorrow
Notifications off during focus sessions
Weekly review completed every Friday

resources.label: Deep Work — Cal Newport
resources.desc: The definitive case for focused, distraction-free work and how to cultivate it.

resources.label: Getting Things Done — David Allen
resources.desc: Trusted productivity system for capturing and processing all your commitments.

resources.label: The Eisenhower Matrix
resources.desc: Visual guide to the 4-quadrant prioritisation framework.

concepts.title: Fixed vs Growth Mindset
concepts.body: Fixed mindset: talent is static, failure is identity. Growth mindset: ability is developed, failure is data. Carol Dweck's research shows growth mindset predicts achievement better than IQ in challenging domains.

concepts.title: Embrace Deliberate Discomfort
concepts.body: If a task feels easy, you're not growing. Deliberately seek projects slightly above your current level. The stretch zone — not the comfort zone — is where skill is built.

concepts.title: Reframe Failure as Feedback
concepts.body: 'I failed' → 'that approach didn't work — here's why.' Keep a failure log: what happened, what I learned, what I'd do differently. Engineers who embrace this compound faster than those who avoid mistakes.

concepts.title: Seek Feedback Actively
concepts.body: Don't wait for performance reviews. Ask after every project: 'What's one thing I could have done better?' Specific, frequent feedback accelerates growth faster than annual reviews.

concepts.title: The Power of 'Not Yet'
concepts.body: Replace 'I can't do X' with 'I can't do X yet.' This tiny word signals to your brain that skill is learnable, not fixed. It maintains motivation through the difficult early phase of any skill.

resources.label: Mindset — Carol Dweck
resources.desc: The original research on fixed vs growth mindset and how it shapes achievement.

resources.label: Grit — Angela Duckworth
resources.desc: How passion + perseverance outperforms raw talent over long time horizons.

concepts.title: Inversion
concepts.body: Instead of asking 'how do I succeed?', ask 'what guarantees failure?' Then avoid those. Charlie Munger: 'All I want to know is where I'm going to die, so I'll never go there.' Apply to systems: what must never happen in production?

concepts.title: First Principles Thinking
concepts.body: Break assumptions until you reach physical or logical truths, then build back up. Musk used this to cut rocket costs 10×. Engineers use it to question 'this is how it's always done' — which is usually the wrong reason.

concepts.title: Second-Order Thinking
concepts.body: Ask 'and then what?' after every decision. First-order: this optimization speeds up the API. Second-order: faster API increases traffic → database becomes the bottleneck. Think two steps ahead before committing.

concepts.title: Pareto Principle (80/20)
concepts.body: 80% of results come from 20% of effort. Identify the 20% of features, fixes, or tasks that produce 80% of value. Cut the rest — or do it last. This is why MVPs beat perfect products in the market.

concepts.title: Occam's Razor
concepts.body: Among competing explanations, prefer the simplest one that fits the facts. In debugging: check environment variables before assuming cache corruption. In architecture: choose the boring technology before the clever one.

concepts.title: Maps Are Not the Territory
concepts.body: Your mental model of a system is not the system. Documentation is not the code. The spec is not the user. Constantly test your assumptions against reality — instruments over intuition.

resources.label: The Great Mental Models — Farnam Street
resources.desc: A curated collection of the most useful mental models across disciplines.

resources.label: Poor Charlie's Almanack
resources.desc: Charlie Munger's worldly wisdom through mental models.

concepts.title: Psychological Safety
concepts.body: Reward 'I don't know' and 'I was wrong'. Punish blame, not mistakes.

concepts.title: Assume Positive Intent
concepts.body: Default to 'they had a reason' instead of 'they're an idiot'. Ask before you assume — 90% of the time it's missing context, not malice.

concepts.title: Shared Ownership
concepts.body: The team ships the feature, not the individual. Rotate ownership of modules so no single person is a bus factor.

resources.label: The Five Dysfunctions of a Team
resources.desc: Patrick Lencioni's model for building trust and accountability.

resources.label: Team Topologies
resources.desc: Organising teams for fast flow of change.

concepts.title: Separate People from Problems
concepts.body: Attack the issue, not the person. 'This approach has a flaw' vs 'your idea is bad'. Keeps conversations productive.

concepts.title: Identify Interests, Not Positions
concepts.body: Ask 'why do you want that?' instead of debating 'what'. Shared interests often exist beneath opposing positions.

concepts.title: Blameless Post-Mortems
concepts.body: Focus on systems and processes that allowed a mistake, not the person who made it. Culture of learning over culture of fear.

concepts.title: The Crucial Conversation Framework
concepts.body: Notice when a conversation turns crucial (stakes + emotions + differing opinions). Create safety, share your path, explore others' paths.

concepts.title: Disagree in Private, Align in Public
concepts.body: Raise concerns before the decision is made, in the right forum. Once the team decides, present a unified front to stakeholders.

resources.label: Crucial Conversations
resources.desc: Tools for high-stakes disagreements.

resources.label: Nonviolent Communication
resources.desc: Empathy-first framework for resolving tension.

resources.label: Getting to Yes
resources.desc: Harvard negotiation project — principled conflict resolution.

concepts.title: Async by Default
concepts.body: Write it down before scheduling a meeting. Loom + Notion + Slack threads handle 80% of communication without synchronous cost.

concepts.title: Overlap Hours Are Sacred
concepts.body: Protect shared working hours for critical discussions and pairing. Everything else is async.

concepts.title: Over-Communicate Context
concepts.body: Remote removes hallway conversations. Add 'why' to every decision. Leave a trail of context in tickets, PRs, and docs.

concepts.title: Camera On for Hard Conversations
concepts.body: Tone is lost in text. Difficult feedback, disagreements, and 1:1s deserve a video call where facial cues are visible.

concepts.title: Document Decisions, Not Just Outcomes
concepts.body: Record what you decided AND why you rejected alternatives. Future teammates won't repeat the same discussions.

resources.label: GitLab Remote Playbook
resources.desc: Industry-leading async remote culture guide.

resources.label: Basecamp — Shape Up
resources.desc: Async-first product development at scale.

concepts.title: Driver / Navigator Pattern
concepts.body: Driver types, navigator thinks ahead and reviews. Swap every 15–25 min with a timer. Never let one person drive for an hour.

concepts.title: Strong-Style Pairing
concepts.body: 'For an idea to go from your head into the computer, it must go through someone else's hands.' The navigator holds all decisions.

concepts.title: Mob Programming
concepts.body: Whole team on one keyboard, one screen, rotating driver every few minutes. Extreme knowledge sharing — nothing gets siloed. Use for onboarding and complex problems.

concepts.title: When to Pair
concepts.body: Complex architecture decisions, debugging gnarly issues, onboarding new team members, or spreading knowledge of a critical system. Don't pair on trivial tasks — it's expensive.

resources.label: Martin Fowler — Pair Programming
resources.desc: Exhaustive guide to pairing styles, benefits, and practical patterns.

concepts.title: Review Intent, Then Implementation
concepts.body: First check: does this PR do the right thing? Then: is it done the right way? A well-written PR that solves the wrong problem should be blocked, not merged and refactored.

concepts.title: Nit vs Blocking
concepts.body: Label comments: 'nit:' (take it or leave it), 'suggestion:' (worth considering), 'blocking:' (must fix). Reviewers who block on every nit slow teams and breed resentment.

concepts.title: Small PRs
concepts.body: A PR with 50 lines gets real review. A PR with 500 lines gets a rubber stamp. Break work into reviewable chunks. Feature flags let you merge incomplete features safely.

concepts.title: Author Responsibility
concepts.body: Don't submit PRs that you wouldn't review. Write a clear description — what, why, how to test. Add screenshots for UI changes. Make the reviewer's job easy.

concepts.title: Deliberate Practice
concepts.body: Reading about programming ≠ practicing programming. Build things at the edge of your ability. Katas, side projects, and open source contributions beat passive consumption.

concepts.title: T-Shaped Skills
concepts.body: Go deep in one area (your specialisation) while maintaining breadth across others (system design, testing, communication). Specialists who understand context are far more valuable.

concepts.title: Public Learning
concepts.body: Write about what you learn — blog posts, tweets, internal RFCs. Teaching forces clarity. It builds your reputation and helps others. The best engineers give more than they take.

concepts.title: The 20% Rule
concepts.body: Protect 20% of your time for learning, experimentation, and technical exploration. If you're always in delivery mode, you'll stagnate. Learning is not optional — it's part of the job.

concepts.title: Sprint Planning
concepts.body: Agree on the sprint goal before picking tickets. The goal is a commitment, not a to-do list. Each item should have a clear Definition of Done. Velocity is a planning tool — not a performance metric.

concepts.title: Daily Standup
concepts.body: What did I do yesterday? What am I doing today? What's blocking me? Keep it under 15 minutes — it's a sync, not a status report. If discussion is needed, take it offline with the relevant people.

concepts.title: Retrospectives
concepts.body: The most underrated ceremony. Keep / Try / Drop format or Start / Stop / Continue. Safety to speak honestly is more important than the format. The retro only has value if actions are tracked and followed through.

concepts.title: Definition of Done
concepts.body: A shared checklist that every piece of work must pass before 'done' is declared: code reviewed, tests passing, deployed to staging, documentation updated. Without DoD, 'done' means nothing.

concepts.title: Kanban vs Scrum
concepts.body: Scrum: time-boxed sprints, roles (PO, SM), planning ceremonies — good for product teams with regular delivery cadence. Kanban: continuous flow, WIP limits — good for ops, support, or maintenance teams. Know which your team actually runs.

concepts.title: The Agile Manifesto Values
concepts.body: Individuals & interactions over processes & tools. Working software over comprehensive documentation. Customer collaboration over contract negotiation. Responding to change over following a plan. Most teams cargo-cult the ceremonies and miss the values.

checklist.label: Sprint goal defined before picking tickets
Definition of Done written and agreed by the team
Retro action items tracked across sprints
Daily standup kept under 15 minutes

resources.desc: The original 4 values and 12 principles — read before anything else.

resources.desc: The official, free Scrum Guide by Schwaber & Sutherland.

resources.desc: An alternative to Scrum for async-first product teams.

concepts.title: First Principles
concepts.body: Strip the problem to physical/logical truths. Rebuild from there. Avoid 'this is how we always do it' as a solution.

concepts.title: 5 Whys
concepts.body: Ask 'why' five times to drive past symptoms to root cause. Pairs well with blameless post-mortems.

concepts.title: Rubber Duck
concepts.body: Explain the problem out loud to a duck (or a colleague who isn't even listening). The act of verbalizing exposes the gap.

concepts.title: Inversion
concepts.body: Instead of 'how do I succeed?', ask 'what guarantees failure?' — then avoid those. Often a faster path to the answer.
